---
title: "Case Selection"
---

V1 uses leader-centered corpora to compare how sacred objects, enemies,
obligation, and sacrifice are organized in political language. The cases are not
treated as identical historical events. They are selected because each can test
some part of the Law of Sacrifice across war, revolution, empire, civil war, and
genocide.

The initial pilot cases for the primary research question are defined here
rather than in the research-question document so that the question can remain
stable as the workbench expands.

## Selection Criteria

Cases should satisfy most of these criteria:

- enough public-domain or open-access material for a balanced core;
- clear connection between political violence and sacred collective objects;
- enough phase coverage to test diachronic development;
- enough register variation to test rhetorical setting;
- enough historical difference to make cross-case comparison meaningful;
- source provenance clear enough for citation and audit.

## V1 Cases

| Case | ID | V1 corpus model | Rationale |
|---|---:|---|---|
| American Revolution | `am-rev` | Washington-anchored collective exception | Tests sacrifice, founding, sacred liberty, and collective exception in revolutionary war language. |
| Napoleon / Napoleonic Wars | `napoleon` | Napoleon-only individual leader corpus | Tests imperial glory, leader-body extension, army/nation fusion, and translation-sensitive sacrifice language. |
| Lincoln / American Civil War | `lincoln` | newly selected Lincoln corpus | Tests body-politic healing, covenant, experiment, providence, war sacrifice, and systematic absence. |
| Hitler / Nazi Germany | `hitler` | Hitler-centered corpus with limited Goebbels/Goering support if needed | Tests the war/genocide boundary, purification fantasy, enemy as bringer of death, and exterminatory body-politic logic. |
| Cross-case synthesis | `x-case` | synthetic synthesis workspace | Not a historical case; receives case-level outputs for comparison, validation, and synthesis. |

## Limits

Case selection does not imply moral equivalence, identical causation, or a
single psychological mechanism. Cross-case comparison should identify both
shared symbolic operations and historically specific differences.

The case set remains provisional until source discovery confirms that each case
can support a balanced core and the necessary validation checks.
